perturbation.original_text: Each of COMPANY and XPLORE agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its respective Representatives, and shall keep a true and correct record of all of such other Party’s Information such Party has provided to its Representatives, but shall not be required to keep records of such other Party’s Information that a Representative accesses via a dataroom.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of COMPANY and XPLORE agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its respective Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause about keeping records of the other Party's information provided to Representatives, it creates an in-text contradiction if a breach occurs because it removes the explicit requirement to track information dissemination to Representatives, making it difficult to enforce the responsibility clause effectively. This makes it impossible to find what representative made such breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Without the prior written consent of the other Party or except as otherwise provided herein, neither COMPANY nor XPLORE will: (i) distribute or disclose to any other person any of the other Party’s Information; (ii) permit any other person to have access to the other Party’s Information; (iii) use the other Party’s Information for any purpose other than the Permitted Use; (iv) (A) use the other Party’s Information in any way that would allow it to obtain a competitive advantage with respect to such Party or (B) reverse engineer such other Party’s Information; or (v) disclose to any other person (A) that discussions, investigations or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between the Parties, or (B) the terms, conditions, status or other facts regarding a possible transaction between the Parties, or (C) that a Party has received Information from the other Party. In addition, the Parties agree that any trade secrets of the other Party which are identified (whether orally or in writing) by the disclosing Party as trade secrets and disclosed to such Party pursuant to this Agreement shall continue to be held confidentially by such Party pursuant to the terms of this Agreement for the duration of the period such trade secrets remain trade secrets under applicable law, notwithstanding any expiration or termination of this Agreement. Notwithstanding the above, each of COMPANY and XPLORE agree that the other Party may disclose the COMPANY Information and the XPLORE Information, respectively, and portions thereof, as well as the information described in clause (iv) of the preceding sentence, to those of such other Party’s directors, officers, employees and, representatives (including financial advisors, lawyers and accountants) of such other Party’s advisors (collectively, “Representatives”) who need to know such Information for the Permitted Use. Each Party will inform its Representatives of the confidential nature of the other Party’s Information and will require its Representatives to abide by the terms of this Agreement and not to disclose the other Party’s Information to any other person. Each of COMPANY and XPLORE agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its respective Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a clause requiring COMPANY and XPLORE to keep records of the other party's information provided to their respective Representatives. The changed text removes this clause. The contradicted text states that each party is responsible for any breach of the agreement by its respective Representatives and requires them to abide by the terms of the agreement. Without the requirement to keep records of the information disseminated to Representatives, it becomes difficult to enforce the responsibility clause and determine which representative caused the breach, thus creating a contradiction. Therefore, the changed text causes a direct conflict making it a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All written COMPANY Information shall be information which is or would generally be considered to be confidential and/or proprietary information, and any information disclosed orally or visually shall be considered confidential Information if it is information which is or would generally be considered to be confidential and/or proprietary information, including if it is reduced to tangible form.
perturbation.changed_text: All written COMPANY Information shall be information which is or would generally be considered to be confidential and/or proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase 'and any information disclosed orally or visually shall be considered confidential Information if it is information which is or would generally be considered to be confidential and/or proprietary information, including if it is reduced to tangible form', the agreement creates ambiguity regarding the confidentiality of orally or visually disclosed information. This omission introduces uncertainty, as it can be argued that only written information is protected, contradicting the intent to protect all forms of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each of COMPANY and XPLORE agrees to hold the other Party’s Information in confidence in accordance with the provisions hereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly included orally and visually disclosed information as confidential. The changed text omits this, creating a contradiction. The 'Contradicted Text' states that both parties agree to hold the other's information in confidence, without specifying the format. The omission in the 'Changed Text' introduces ambiguity as it could be argued that only written information is now protected, which directly contradicts the agreement to hold *all* of the other party's information in confidence, as per the 'Contradicted Text'. This aligns with the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: If at any time during the eighteen month period referred to in the preceding paragraph (I) either Party enters into a definitive agreement providing for a Combination (as defined below) or becomes the subject of a tender or exchange offer which, if consummated, would constitute a Combination is commenced for securities of either Party, then upon the occurrence of any such event, the restrictions on the other Party set forth in the preceding paragraph (I) shall terminate and all other provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect in accordance with the terms hereof. A “Combination” shall mean a transaction in which (i) a person or “group” (within the meaning of Section 13(d)(3) of the Exchange Act) acquires, directly or indirectly, securities representing fifty percent (50%) or more of the voting power of the outstanding securities of such Party or properties or all or substantially all the assets of such Party and its subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: If at any time during the eighteen month period referred to in the preceding paragraph (I) either Party enters into a definitive agreement providing for a Combination (as defined below) or becomes the subject of a tender or exchange offer which, if consummated, would constitute a Combination is commenced for securities of either Party, then upon the occurrence of any such event, the restrictions on the other Party set forth in the preceding paragraph (I) shall terminate and all other provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect in accordance with the terms hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of “Combination,” the clause about termination of restrictions becomes vague and unenforceable. The contradiction arises because the agreement refers to a 'Combination' that triggers termination of restrictions but doesn't specify what a 'Combination' is, leading to disputes over whether the termination clause should be activated.
perturbation.contradicted_text: If at any time during the eighteen month period referred to in the preceding paragraph (I) either Party enters into a definitive agreement providing for a Combination (as defined below) or becomes the subject of a tender or exchange offer which, if consummated, would constitute a Combination is commenced for securities of either Party, then upon the occurrence of any such event, the restrictions on the other Party set forth in the preceding paragraph (I) shall terminate and all other provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and effect in accordance with the terms hereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Combination,' which is essential for understanding when the restrictions on the other party terminate. The changed text removes this definition. The contradicted text still references 'Combination' as a trigger for termination, but without a definition, its meaning is ambiguous and unenforceable, creating a direct contradiction. The contract now refers to a term without defining it, leading to conflicting interpretations of when the termination clause is activated as described in the Perturbation Explanation. The omission of the definition directly contradicts the usage of the term in the rest of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of complying with the obligations set forth herein, each of COMPANY and XPLORE shall use efforts fully commensurate with those that it employs for the protection of its privileged and confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of complying with the obligations set forth herein, each of COMPANY and XPLORE shall use commercially reasonable efforts.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the phrase 'efforts fully commensurate with those that it employs for the protection of its privileged and confidential Information' with 'commercially reasonable efforts,' the agreement introduces ambiguity and weakens the standard of care. The omission of a clear benchmark for the efforts required contradicts the implicit intent to provide a high level of protection for the confidential information, now only subject to the less stringent "commercially reasonable efforts." This creates uncertainty about what level of effort is actually required and could be seen as contradictory to the agreements intent to protect such info
perturbation.contradicted_text: For purposes of complying with the obligations set forth herein, each of COMPANY and XPLORE shall use commercially reasonable efforts. Each Party agrees that neither it nor any of its subsidiaries or affiliates has been granted any license, copyright or other similar right or privilege with respect to any of the Information or other information provided by or on behalf of the other Party. Each Party hereby acknowledges and confirms that all existing and future intellectual property rights relating to the other Party’s Information are the exclusive property of such Party. Each Party agrees that it will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the other Party’s Information. All intellectual property rights relating to any drawings, documents and work carried out by any Party (whether past, present or future) using the other Party’s Information will belong to and will vest in the other Party. Each Party agrees that it will do all such things and execute all documents necessary to enable the other Party to obtain, defend or enforce its rights in such drawings, documents and work.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified 'efforts fully commensurate with those that it employs for the protection of its privileged and confidential Information', setting a high standard. The changed text lowers this standard to 'commercially reasonable efforts.' The contradicted text repeats the 'commercially reasonable efforts' language from the changed text. Although the contradicted text does not cause the contradiction, by changing the initial high standard to a lower standard, the contract now has conflicting requirements regarding the level of effort required to protect confidential information. This creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Such return, however, does not abrogate the continuing obligations of XPLORE under this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, one copy of the COMPANY Information and the notes, correspondence, analyses, documents or other records containing COMPANY Information may be retained by XPLORE’s in-house or external attorneys to prevent possible future misunderstandings regarding the scope of the disclosure and XPLORE will not be required to destroy electronic back-up versions of the COMPANY Information to the extent such destruction is not reasonably practical; provided that any COMPANY Information retained on routine computer system back-up tapes, disks or other back-up storage devices shall not be used, disclosed or otherwise recovered from such back-up devices unless required for regulatory purposes or legal process.
perturbation.changed_text: Such return, however, does not abrogate the continuing obligations of XPLORE under this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, one copy of the COMPANY Information and the notes, correspondence, analyses, documents or other records containing COMPANY Information may be retained by XPLORE’s in-house attorneys to prevent possible future misunderstandings regarding the scope of the disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause specifying the permissible use of retained copies of confidential information and the exception for external attorneys, it narrows down the permissible reasons for keeping such copy, which contradicts the clause that states 'Such return, however, does not abrogate the continuing obligations of XPLORE under this Agreement'. Due to the restrictions to retain a copy of such infomration.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the request of COMPANY, XPLORE will return or destroy (at XPLORE’S option) all COMPANY Information and any notes, correspondence, analyses, documents or other records containing COMPANY Information, including all copies thereof, then in the possession of XPLORE or its Representatives. Such return, however, does not abrogate the continuing obligations of XPLORE under this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, one copy of the COMPANY Information and the notes, correspondence, analyses, documents or other records containing COMPANY Information may be retained by XPLORE’s in-house attorneys to prevent possible future misunderstandings regarding the scope of the disclosure and XPLORE will not be required to destroy electronic back-up versions of the COMPANY Information to the extent such destruction is not reasonably practical; provided that any COMPANY Information retained on routine computer system back-up tapes, disks or other back-up storage devices shall not be used, disclosed or otherwise recovered from such back-up devices unless required for regulatory purposes or legal process. Any destruction will be certified by an officer of XPLORE, and all retained information shall remain subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for both in-house and external attorneys to retain a copy of the company information, with a specific clause outlining the permissible use of the retained information and an exception for electronic back-up versions. The changed text removes the reference to external attorneys and the specific conditions for use and retention, essentially narrowing the scope of who can retain the information and under what conditions. This creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, because the initial agreement allows for a broader range of retention conditions, while the changed text restricts them, causing an in-text contradiction.
